metadata.title: Antarctica — the Treaty regime, the frozen claims, the mining ban, and the creeping resource/base competition (both sides, dated)
metadata.status: the Antarctic Treaty, the Madrid Protocol mining ban, the territorial claims, China's base expansion, and Russia's reported surveys are FACT (treaty texts, govt/research records, news of record); 'research vs prospecting' is presented both ways
metadata.frame: Regional block: Antarctica as the last commons under a fraying consensus. The Antarctic Treaty (1959) demilitarizes the continent + freezes 7 territorial claims for science; the 1991 Madrid Protocol bans mining (reviewable from ~2048). But competition creeps: China opened its 5th station (Qinling, 2024); Russia reported large hydrocarbon 'seismic surveys' (2020s) read by some as prospecting; krill/toothfish fishing + biosecurity pressure mount. Attaches to China, Russia, UK, Argentina, Chile, US_Government.

the_treaty: The Antarctic Treaty (signed 1959, in force 1961) reserves Antarctica for peaceful/scientific use, DEMILITARIZES it, bans nuclear tests, and FREEZES the 7 territorial claims (no new claims, none recognized/renounced). The Antarctic Treaty System (ATS) governs by consensus. grade: fact.
the_mining_ban: The 1991 Madrid Protocol (Protocol on Environmental Protection) bans all mineral-resource activity except scientific research — designating Antarctica a 'natural reserve.' It can be reviewed/modified from ~2048 (50 years after entry into force), the date often cited as the system's stress test. grade: fact.
the_claims: Seven states hold territorial claims: UK, Argentina, Chile (OVERLAPPING on the Antarctic Peninsula), plus Norway, Australia, France, New Zealand. The US and Russia recognize no claims but reserve the right to claim. The UK/Argentina/Chile overlap mirrors the Falklands/Malvinas friction. grade: fact.
the_competition: The creeping contest, both ways: CHINA opened its 5th research station (Qinling, on the Ross Sea, 2024) — dual-use concerns (potential SIGINT) vs stated science; RUSSIA's Rosgeo reported very large hydrocarbon 'seismic surveys' in the Weddell Sea (2020s) — framed by Russia as RESEARCH but read by critics as PROSPECTING that would breach the spirit of the mining ban; fishing pressure (krill for aquaculture/supplements, Patagonian toothfish) + tourism + biosecurity stress the CCAMLR fisheries regime. grade: bases/surveys are fact; 'research vs prospecting / dual-use' is the contested read.
the_honest_reading: Antarctica's governance (the 1959 Treaty + the 1991 mining ban) still holds, but the consensus is fraying at the edges: overlapping UK/Argentina/Chile claims, China's growing base network (dual-use questions), Russia's hydrocarbon 'surveys' (research-vs-prospecting), and mounting fishing/biosecurity pressure — with the ~2048 mining-ban review looming as the real test. Facts are dated; the research-vs-resource-interest reading is presented both ways; no unitary national 'mind' is imputed. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: Secretariat of the Antarctic Treaty — the Treaty + Madrid Protocol

edges.raw_instrument: 1959 Treaty: peaceful/scientific use, demilitarized, no nuclear tests, claims FROZEN (consensus ATS governance)
1991 Madrid Protocol: bans mineral activity ('natural reserve'); reviewable from ~2048 (the stress test)
5 research stations (Qinling on the Ross Sea, 2024) — dual-use/SIGINT concerns vs stated science
Rosgeo reported large hydrocarbon 'seismic surveys' (Weddell Sea, 2020s) — 'research' vs prospecting (mining-ban spirit) contested
territorial claim (overlapping with Argentina + Chile on the Peninsula)
territorial claim (overlaps UK/Chile; mirrors Falklands/Malvinas friction)
McMurdo/Amundsen-Scott stations; recognizes no claims but reserves the right (as does Russia)
